original_abstract:
Coordination complexes with a $d^6$ valence electron configuration, such as those of Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III), are fundamental to modern lighting, solar energy conversion, and photocatalysis. However, achieving comparable photophysical performance using abundant first-row transition metals has remained a significant challenge. While most efforts toward sustainable alternatives have centered on Fe(II), we demonstrate that the isoelectronic Cr(0) center enables excited-state lifetimes and photoluminescence quantum yields that surpass those of any previously reported first-row $d^6$ complex. 

By employing specific molecular design strategies, we synthesized Cr(0) complexes whose metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states exhibit luminescent properties competitive with benchmark Os(II) polypyridines. These robust photophysical characteristics allow these earth-abundant complexes to function as powerful reductants in photoredox catalysis, as evidenced by the efficient execution of challenging chemical reductions under low-energy red light. This work shows that appropriate structural tailoring can overcome the traditional limitations of 3d metals, opening new avenues for sustainable photophysics and chemistry.
humanized_abstract:
Coordination complexes containing a $d^6$ $metal$ center such as Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III) are ubiquitously found in many applications related to lighting, solar energy conversion and photocatalysis. Realizing similar photophysical properties in abundant first row transition metals has remained an open question. While most recent investigations have concentrated on Fe(II), we now demonstrate that the isoelectronic Cr(0) metal center allows for much longer-lived excited states as well as enhanced photoluminescence quantum yields relative to all previously reported first row $d^6$ complexes.

Cr(0) complexes were prepared by strategic molecular design and their photophysics as the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited state was studied and characterized. Unexpectedly, Cr(0) complexes showed luminescence within a few percent of maximum efficiency compared to known benchmark compounds composed of an osmium(II) centre and showed robust photophysical properties. They proved to act as high power reductants in photoredox catalysis by enabling challenging reductions to proceed upon low energy (red light) irradiation. In this work, by strategic molecular design, we are able to bypass the limitations of 3d metals and show the capabilities of earth abundant elements in terms of photophysics and catalysis.

document.output:

Realizing similar photophysical properties in abundant first row transition metals has remained an open question. While most recent investigations have concentrated on Fe(II), we now demonstrate that the isoelectronic Cr(0) metal center allows for much longer-lived excited states as well as enhanced photoluminescence quantum yields relative to all previously reported first row $d^6$ complexes.

Cr(0) complexes were prepared by strategic molecular design and their photophysics as the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited state was studied and characterized. Unexpectedly, Cr(0) complexes showed luminescence within a few percent of maximum efficiency compared to known benchmark compounds composed of an osmium(II) centre and showed robust photophysical properties. document.input:
Coordination complexes with a $d^6$ valence electron configuration, such as those of Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III), are fundamental to modern lighting, solar energy conversion, and photocatalysis. However, achieving comparable photophysical performance using abundant first-row transition metals has remained a significant challenge. While most efforts toward sustainable alternatives have centered on Fe(II), we demonstrate that the isoelectronic Cr(0) center enables excited-state lifetimes and photoluminescence quantum yields that surpass those of any previously reported first-row $d^6$ complex. 

By employing specific molecular design strategies, we synthesized Cr(0) complexes whose metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states exhibit luminescent properties competitive with benchmark Os(II) polypyridines. These robust photophysical characteristics allow these earth-abundant complexes to function as powerful reductants in photoredox catalysis, as evidenced by the efficient execution of challenging chemical reductions under low-energy red light. This work shows that appropriate structural tailoring can overcome the traditional limitations of 3d metals, opening new avenues for sustainable photophysics and chemistry.
